mnemonics.title: Waterfall Phases
mnemonics.content: P DET I V M - Planning, Design, Implementation, Verification, Maintenance (in order)

mnemonics.title: Scrum Roles
mnemonics.content: POS - Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team (in any team)

mnemonics.title: V-Model Testing Levels
mnemonics.content: UISA - Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance (testing in V-Model order)

mnemonics.title: Agile Values
mnemonics.content: IVCR - Individuals & Interactions, Working Software, Customer Collaboration, Responding to Change (over their alternatives)

mnemonics.title: Spiral Model Cycles
mnemonics.content: PRER - Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Review (repeating cycle)

mnemonics.title: SDLC Phases
mnemonics.content: PAD TI - Planning, Analysis, Design, Testing, Implementation (maintenance throughout)

acronyms.usage: The complete process of developing software from conception to retirement
Document created during requirements phase containing all functional and non-functional requirements
DevOps practices for automating integration and deployment of code changes
Methodology emphasizing quick prototyping and iterative development
Final testing phase where end users validate the software against their needs
Tools used to automate software development activities
The smallest product with core features for early adopter feedback

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a waterfall flowing downward - each phase cascades into the next. Draw phases as connected boxes going down, with no going back arrows.
Draw development phases on the left slope going down, and corresponding testing phases on the right slope going up, meeting at the bottom (implementation).
Draw as a spiral starting from center, expanding outward. Each loop represents a cycle with 4 quadrants: Plan, Risk, Develop, Evaluate.
Draw a circular cycle showing: Sprint Planning → Daily Scrums → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective → Sprint Planning (repeating)
Show multiple circles (sprints) side by side, each containing: Plan → Build → Test → Review, with each producing a working increment

analogies.analogy: Like building a house: first architect draws plans, then foundation is laid, then walls are built, then roof, then painting - each step must be complete before next.
Like building with LEGO bricks - create small pieces, test they fit, add more pieces, keep improving based on how it looks and works.
Like writing a first draft of an essay - write, review for problems, revise, write more, review again, keep spiraling toward final product.
Like a factory assembly line where same team builds and operates the machines - no handoff between manufacturing and operations.
Like a clay model for a car design - used to visualize and test ideas before committing to expensive mass production.
Like a sprint in a relay race - a fixed distance (time) where you run your hardest to deliver the baton (product) to the next runner.
